prompt: Make this better: 'Five things I wish I knew before starting a company: (1) Cash is oxygen. (2) Hire slow, fire fast. (3) Talk to customers. (4) Culture is set early. (5) Take care of your health. All five cost me dearly to learn.'
rubric: Excellent editing recognizes that all five items are interchangeable platitudes the reader has seen a hundred times, and forces at least the strongest one into a specific, costly, falsifiable scene — a real number, a named decision, the actual moment the lesson bit — rather than just polishing the slogans; it should also question whether five generic bullets beat one hard-won story, and refuse to let 'cost me dearly' stay an unredeemed claim with no evidence of what was actually lost.

prompt: Improve this post: 'Gratitude changed my life. When you focus on what you have instead of what you lack, everything shifts. I try to write down three things I'm grateful for every morning. Highly recommend it.'
rubric: A top-tier edit attacks the central emptiness — 'changed my life' and 'everything shifts' assert a transformation the reader has no reason to believe — by demanding one concrete before/after (what specifically was different) and a non-generic, even counterintuitive observation about the practice (when it fails, what almost nobody admits about it), instead of the frictionless self-help cadence ending in 'highly recommend'; merely tightening the prose while leaving it a testimonial about nothing is a failing edit.

prompt: Help me sharpen this draft: 'Failure is the best teacher. My startup didn't work out, but I learned so much. Every entrepreneur should embrace failure as part of the journey. Onward to the next one.'
rubric: The bar is to expose that this draft says literally nothing — it names no failure, no lesson, and no cost — and the great edit replaces the 'failure is the best teacher / embrace the journey' clichés (which a sharp editor should note are often false) with the one specific thing that actually broke, what the writer concretely now does differently, and an honest reckoning that resists the triumphant 'onward'; an answer that keeps the inspirational arc and just sharpens word choice misses the point.

prompt: Make this LinkedIn post stronger: 'Reflecting on 10 years in sales. The biggest lesson? People buy from people they trust. Relationships are everything. Grateful for every client who believed in me along the way.'
rubric: Strong editing refuses to let 'people buy from people they trust' and 'relationships are everything' stand as the takeaway from a decade, since they're the most predictable sentences in sales — it presses for a specific mechanism or contrarian truth ten years actually taught (a deal trust lost, what trust concretely looked like in one transaction, when relationships did NOT close the sale) and cuts the obligatory gratitude sign-off unless it earns its place; polishing it into a smoother but equally generic LinkedIn-voice post is not an improvement.

prompt: Improve this: 'Burnout is real. I pushed too hard for too long and hit a wall last year. Now I prioritize rest and boundaries. If you're grinding nonstop, this is your sign to slow down.'
rubric: An excellent edit treats this as nearly content-free advice ('prioritize rest and boundaries,' 'slow down') wrapped in a stock burnout arc, and demands the specifics that would make it credible and useful — what 'hitting a wall' actually looked like, what a 'boundary' concretely means in practice (the meeting declined, the rule held), and ideally an honest complication (rest alone didn't fix it / the grind had a real reason) — while dropping the 'this is your sign' influencer tic; a sympathetic but vague rewrite that still tells strangers to 'slow down' has not been improved.
